Cape Verde.status: Two creditable draws. A win here puts them in strong control of 2nd place.

Saudi Arabia.status: Needs a win, and likely a big GD swing, to have a real shot at advancing.

CPV.CRITICAL: Remarkably disciplined low-possession side — only 1 foul in 90 minutes against Spain. Two excellent defensive results (0-0 vs Spain, 2-2 vs Uruguay) despite minimal possession both times.

SAU.NOTE: Classic RULE10 disengagement pattern: 11 fouls in a competitive 1-1 draw vs Uruguay, only 2 fouls while being blown out 0-4 by Spain. Today's match is far more competitive than the Spain game, so expect the higher (11-foul) rate to be more representative.

context_flags: EVEN_MATCHUP: FTR is almost a true 3-way pick'em (CPV 36.9% / SAU 34.4% / Draw 29.0%) — neither team is a clear favorite, unlike both teams' games against Spain.
RULE8_WATCH: HT Draw priced at 46.52% — elevated, consistent with a cagey, low-event match expected between two evenly-matched, defensively solid sides.

SAU_RULE10_FOUL_SWING: Saudi Arabia's foul rate is highly game-state dependent (11 when competitive, 2 when blown out). Today's even matchup should pull them toward the higher rate, not their 2-match average.

CPV_GENUINE_DISCIPLINE: Cape Verde's low foul count appears to be a real team trait (low fouls in BOTH a blowout-against scenario and a competitive draw), not just blowout disengagement — trust it more than a typical low-foul read.
BOTH_LOW_SOT_TEAMS: Cape Verde (2.5 avg) and Saudi Arabia (2.0 avg) are both modest shot-generation teams — supports a generally low-event match across SOT-based props.

questions.text: Will Cape Verde commit more fouls than Saudi Arabia?
questions.reasoning: Normal-approx on raw averages (CPV 2.5 vs SAU 6.5) gives P=0.17. Cape Verde's discipline looks like a genuine team trait (low fouls even in a competitive draw), while Saudi Arabia's foul rate should rise toward their competitive-match level (11) in this even fixture. Small upward nudge from the raw number for uncertainty, but direction is clear.

questions.text: Will both teams have at least 1 shot on target in the second half?
questions.reasoning: Half-split Poisson off full-match ESPN SOT averages (CPV λ_2H=1.25 → P(≥1)=0.71; SAU λ_2H=1.0 → P(≥1)=0.63). Joint ≈ 0.45.

questions.text: Will a penalty kick be awarded in the match?
Will Cape Verde be caught offside 2 or more times?
questions.reasoning: CPV offsides were 3 (vs Spain, low-possession counter-attacking) and 0 (vs Uruguay, more controlled approach where they scored twice). λ=1.5 → raw Poisson P(≥2)=0.44. High variance (n=2, opposite patterns), and today's even matchup against a non-high-press Saudi Arabia should produce fewer high-risk transition forays than the Spain game did. Shade down from raw.

questions.text: Will Saudi Arabia have more shots on target than Cape Verde in the second half?
questions.reasoning: Half-split Skellam (SAU λ_2H=1.0, CPV λ_2H=1.25) gives P(SAU>CPV)=0.30. Cape Verde has actually out-generated Saudi Arabia on SOT across the tournament so far (2.5 vs 2.0 avg) despite far less possession in their matches — a real, if modest, signal.

questions.text: Will Cape Verde win the match?
questions.reasoning: Smarkets FTR: Cape Verde 36.90%. Direct tier-1 market in a near-3-way-even fixture.

questions.text: Will Cape Verde score in the second half?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: CPV 2H over 0.5 goals = 49.49%. Essentially a coin-flip — trust it as-is.

questions.text: Will Ryan Mendes score or assist a goal (excluding own goals)?
questions.reasoning: Illiquid offer (0.365, adjusted ×0.945=0.345). Mendes is Cape Verde's primary attacking threat; no strong RULE15 case since Cape Verde isn't an extreme underdog (36.9% win prob) in this fixture. Trust the market closely.

questions.text: Will Salem Al-Dawsari score a goal (excluding own goals)?
questions.reasoning: Illiquid offer (0.25, adjusted ×0.945=0.236). Saudi Arabia has scored only 1 goal in 2 WC2026 matches; Al-Dawsari is their most dangerous attacker but the team's overall scoring output has been very low. Trust the market near-raw.

questions.text: Will Saudi Arabia receive more cards than Cape Verde?
questions.reasoning: Card averages are EQUAL on paper (SAU 1.5, CPV 1.5), so a naive read is a coin-flip — but the symmetric Poisson comparison (λ=1.5 vs 1.5) already gives 0.38 once tie-probability mass is removed. Context confirms the same direction: both teams' card counts are NOT driven by foul volume but by match competitiveness — SAU got MORE cards in their blowout loss (2, despite only 2 fouls) than in their competitive draw (1, despite 11 fouls), while CPV got MORE cards in their competitive draw (2) than their disciplined draw vs Spain (1). Today's fixture is a competitive, even match — exactly the scenario where CPV's card rate (2) historically exceeds SAU's (1).

strongest_picks: Q1: CPV more fouls than SAU = 0.22 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — genuine discipline trait vs SAU's RULE10 game-state-dependent fouling)
Q3: Penalty awarded = 0.25 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — direct market)
Q6: Cape Verde wins = 0.37 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — direct market in a near-even fixture)

Q1_CPV_more_fouls.note: Biggest win of the match. CPV's discipline-as-genuine-trait read (not just blowout disengagement) held up cleanly in a competitive 0-0.

Q2_both_SOT_2H.note: Second-biggest loss. Both teams found at least 1 2H SOT in a cagey match — the half-split Poisson (0.45) undershot; crowd's 66% was closer.

Q4_CPV_2plus_offsides.note: THE KEY MISS. Cape Verde sat on exactly 1 offside for almost the entire match — consistent with our cagey-match read — then picked up a 2nd in stoppage time pushing for a late winner in a still-0-0, must-have-points game. Root cause: we shaded DOWN from the raw Poisson (0.44) to 0.32 based on a 'fewer transition forays in an even matchup' narrative. The raw number was closer to truth than our shaded one, AND we missed a distinct pattern — late-match desperation in a scoreless draw between two teams that both need a result raises stoppage-time offside risk, similar in spirit to chasing-a-deficit (BRA-HAI, POR-UZB, TUR-PAR) but triggered by a tied scoreline + mutual must-win pressure rather than an actual deficit. See updated STEPS_FOR_HIGH_POINTS.md entry.

Q7_CPV_scores_2H.note: Exact tie with crowd, correct side.

Q10_SAU_more_cards.note: Our 'competitive match favors Cape Verde's higher card rate' read was wrong — Saudi Arabia picked up more cards in this competitive 0-0 instead.

key_confirmations: Q1 and Q4 are a direct contrast worth studying together: Q1 (trusting a real discipline signal near-raw) won big; Q4 (shading a real signal DOWN based on a plausible-sounding narrative) lost big. Same match, same team, opposite lesson outcomes depending on whether we trusted the data or a story about the data.

NEW PATTERN CANDIDATE: 'mutual must-win stalemate' late-match risk — when two teams that BOTH need a win are still scoreless deep into the match, treat stoppage-time event risk (offsides, cards, corners from late desperation) as elevated, not suppressed, even in an otherwise cagey match.
